Tafseer:
The nature of the address:
Although the address 'Have you not seen' (alam tara) is singular in terms of wording, it is mostly used to address a group, and this style of address directly focuses on each individual within the group. The addressee here is the Quraysh. They are directed to reflect on how their Lord dealt with the owners of the elephant—asking if they have not seen it. Note that a long period of time had not passed since the incident of the owners of the elephant. The birth of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) occurred during the Year of the Elephant; therefore, at the time of the Surah's revelation, there must have been many people who witnessed the event with their own eyes, or heard of it through such continuous reports that it was equivalent to witnessing. For this reason, the address is in its highly appropriate place here.
Who were the owners of the elephant? The Qur'an does not present any details here as to who they were, where they came from, or what their objective was, but merely points to their end briefly. This brevity is because the addressee group was well aware of the event. Describing them as 'the owners of the elephant' was sufficient to understand that it refers to the Abyssinian ruler of Yemen, Abraha, whose invading army was accompanied by massive elephants. Since the experience of an army with elephants was new to the Arabs, they remembered this invasion by this name, which expresses its gravity.
Whether there was a single elephant or more, the wording can accommodate both, but describing them as 'the owners of the elephant' (as-hab-ul-feel) suggests there were multiple elephants, and reports also state that a group of elephants accompanied the army, which greatly increased its strength and terror.
Abraha and his character: Although some historians have described Abraha as a moderate ruler, his life events do not support this assumption, but show clearly that he was an opportunistic, treacherous, and highly fanatic Christian. He betrayed the king of Abyssinia, with whose armies he had conquered Yemen—as documented in history—but there is no need to discuss it here. After conquering Yemen, he not only killed its Jewish king, but completely eradicated Jews and Judaism from there.
Abraha's plot and its failure: In his fanaticism, he planned to turn the Arabs into Christians. To execute this, he constructed a grand cathedral in Sanaa, the capital of Yemen, and wrote to the Negus of Abyssinia, under whose authority he ruled Yemen: 'I have constructed a cathedral the like of which has never been seen by the eye of the heaven. I want to divert the Arabs' Hajj toward it, and demolish their temple in Mecca.' Following this, to create a pretext for attacking the Kaaba, he rumored that a certain Arab had desecrated his cathedral. This event seems completely false, as the Arabs were brave and did not commit such cheap acts; but even if true, an individual's act cannot justify taking retribution from the entire nation, let alone demolishing their temple. However, to incite Christians and gain the support of the Negus, this lie was widely publicized, and a massive army of sixty thousand men, accompanied by nine to ten elephants, was gathered to attack Mecca.

But Allah Almighty completely devastated and nullified all of Abraha's plans. Describing his plans as 'kayd' (plotting/plot) has a clear reason, as we noted—namely: a highly tyrannical invasion was justified by fabricating a highly baseless accusation, but his plot has other aspects as well, to which our teacher pointed in his commentary, writing:
'1. He attacked during the sacred months, assuming the Arabs would avoid war and bloodshed during these months.'
'2. He tried to enter Mecca at a time when the Meccans were busy performing the rituals of Hajj with other Arabs.'
'3. He chose the days of the stay at Mina to attack, assuming the Arabs would be busy with sacrifices at Mina or would be returning home exhausted.'
The divine arrangement to nullify Abraha's plots: The summary of the arrangement Allah Almighty made to nullify his plots was described by our teacher in these words:
'1. His army was stopped in the valley of Muhassir.'
'2. The Arabs used the stones of Muhassir as weapons, pelting them with stones, as detailed next.'
'3. Furthermore, Allah Almighty unleashed a wind carrying stones (hasib) upon these enemies of the Kaaba, which devastated them completely.'
This 'hasib' (stone-carrying wind) was mentioned by the eyewitnesses of the event, and Ibn Hisham and others recorded these testimonies in their books. Our teacher discussed this in detail, and we present only two examples. The famous poet, Abu Qays, mentioning some of the wonders of divine power regarding this event, described 'hasib' as:
'Then He unleashed upon them a hasib from their Lord, wrapping them up like straw.'
Similarly, Sayfi bin Amir also mentioned 'saf' and 'hasib': (Footnote 1) he wrote:
'When they crossed the valley of Na'man, the hosts of God turned them back through a wind of saf and hasib.'
_____
Footnote 1: 'Saf' is also used for a violent wind, like 'hasib', with only a difference in degree.

Metaphorical description of the destruction of Abraha's hosts: This describes the destruction, devastation, and helplessness of Abraha's hosts metaphorically—showing that Allah Almighty devastated them by His perfect power in such a manner that no one was left to bury their bodies. They remained in the field, and Allah Almighty sent flesh-eating birds upon them to tear and eat their flesh, cleansing the valley of Mecca of their stench. Unleashing birds upon the enemy is a recognized metaphor for defeat and devastation. Arab poets boasted in their verses: 'When our armies attack the enemy, flesh-eating birds accompany us, knowing they will feed on the flesh of the defeated.' The account of David and Goliath in the Torah also states: when David stood to fight him and answered his arrogant words, Goliath said in anger: 'Come, let me feed your flesh to the birds of heaven.' But Allah Almighty gave David victory, and his own flesh was eaten by birds.
Ababeel: 'Ababeel' does not refer to the species of birds commonly known as ababeel. This word refers to herds of horses or flocks of birds. There is a difference as to whether it is singular or plural; some say it has no singular, while others regard it as the plural of 'ibbalah'. Here, it refers to the flocks of birds that gather to feed on the bodies of the dead.
'He sent upon them' (arsala 'alayhim) contains the meaning of unleashing, pointing to the helpless state of the owners of the elephant—showing that no one was there to bury them; hence, the birds had complete freedom to feed on them.

A common misunderstanding of commentators: Finally, it is stated how much share the Arabs had in destroying this army and how much belonged to His power: you were pelting them with stones of clay, and He turned them into devoured straw. That is: compared to this massive army, your defense was indeed weak, but Allah Almighty, through His unseen help, made your weak defense so effective that they became like devoured straw.
Generally, commentators have asserted that the Quraysh did not defend against Abraha at all, but their leader, Abdul-Muttalib, took the people to the mountains, leaving the Kaaba to God's care, assuming He would protect His House Himself. In their view, the subject of 'tarmeehim' (pelting them) is 'tayran ababeel' (birds in flocks)—meaning the birds pelted them with stones and devastated them. Although all commentators agree on this, it is incorrect for several reasons:
1. There is no doubt that the Quraysh went to the mountains, but this does not mean they abandoned defense completely; rather, this was the only possible method of defense against Abraha's massive army, so they adopted it, and Allah Almighty—in accordance with His law of helping those who perform their duties to the best of their ability—helped the Quraysh.
We noted above that Abraha's army was sixty thousand men, accompanied by a group of elephants. Facing such an army in an open field with swords and spears was impossible for the Quraysh, who could not have gathered more than ten to twenty thousand men even with their best efforts; hence, they chose the best war policy—namely, defending from the mountains. This was similar to the policy the Muslims adopted during the Battle of the Trench, when they dug a trench around Medina and defended from within it.
2. The claim that the Quraysh did not defend at all is contrary to history as well as their honor and pride. All historians agree that the Arab tribes along the route defended against Abraha's army to the best of their ability, preferring defeat over allowing the enemy to pass easily to attack the Kaaba. Only one tribe, Banu Thaqif, acted contrary to this, and one individual, Abu Righal, showed Abraha's army the way to Mecca, for which his name was disgraced and his grave was pelted with stones as a curse for ages. Consider that when small tribes defended with such courage, how could the Quraysh show such weakness as to allow him to take control of the Kaaba easily? And if they had indeed allowed him without any defense, why did they pelt Abu Righal's grave with stones? Thus, the claim that they hid in the mountains without any defense is incorrect. The honor of the Quraysh is famous; they never showed weakness in minor matters, so how could they show such weakness regarding the Kaaba, upon which their entire status depended? After the Kaaba, what did they have left for which they would save their lives by hiding in the mountains!
3. According to those who accuse the Quraysh of this weakness, the lesson of this Surah is that Allah protects His House Himself, even if His guardians run away. Thus, when they fled to the mountains, Allah sent the birds to pelt them with stones and destroy them. If this is the lesson, it is contrary to the divine law. Allah's law is not that His servants sit at home like the Children of Israel, saying: (So go, you and your Lord, and fight. Indeed, we are remaining here) (Al-Ma'idah, 5:24), and He wins the field for them to rule. If He did so, why did He not do so for the Children of Israel, whom He punished by leaving them to wander in the desert for forty years? The divine law explained in the Qur'an is that He helps those who stand to perform their duties, even if they are few and lack resources. Thus, regarding the Kaaba, the Qur'an states in Surah Al-Baqarah, Surah At-Tawbah, and Surah Al-Hajj that we are responsible for protecting and defending its freedom, and He will help us. It has nowhere stated that He will protect it through birds while you do nothing. The Quraysh did what was in their power, and although their defense was weak, Allah Almighty added His power through the stone-carrying wind, turning the enemy into devoured straw. At the Battle of Badr, the Prophet threw a handful of dust toward the Quraysh, which became a storm, and Allah explained its significance as: (And you threw not, [O Muhammad], when you threw, but it was Allah Who threw) (Al-Anfal, 8:17).
4. The supplication of Abdul-Muttalib on Mount Hira does not show that they abandoned defense completely, but contains the same pride and trust in Allah that was manifest in the Prophet's supplication at the Battle of Badr. Such a supplication is not offered by one who flees, but by one who, though anxious, trusts in his Lord. Those who have interpreted it as fleeing have shown a lack of understanding. When I read it, I find the tone of a war song, and it smells of faith. Read these verses with attention; they contain great warmth and a highly effective appeal to Allah's honor:
'O Allah, a man protects his home, so protect Your home!'
'Their cross and their power should never overcome Your power!'
'If You wish to leave them to our Qiblah, then do as You wish!'
Can such a person of honor be assumed to flee the field?
Thus, the opinion that the Quraysh fled is baseless. 'Tarmeehim' (pelting them) refers to the Quraysh who are addressed in 'Have you not seen'. The verb 'tarmee' is grammatically incorrect for birds, as they can drop stones from their beaks but this cannot be described as 'pelting' (which requires force). Thus, those who believed the birds pelted them had to argue that they dropped stones of the size of peas which pierced through the riders and the elephants. While they showed the effect of the stones, this cannot be described as 'pelting'.
'Sijjeel': 'bi-hijaratim-min-sijjeel' - 'Sijjeel' is the arabized form of the Persian 'sang-i-gil' (baked clay). Translating it as pebbles is correct. We noted that the Quraysh's defense was weak, as they could not face them in an open field, and therefore defended by pelting them with stones from the mountains. To clarify this, the Qur'an described the stones as 'pebbles of baked clay'.

This describes Allah's power: although your defense was weak, when you stood to defend, He sent His help in accordance with His law, turning them into devoured straw. Naming something after its end is a common Arabic style, and 'ka-'asfim-ma'kool' (like devoured straw) is of this nature.
Note that while 'pelting' is attributed to the addressees, turning them into devoured straw is described as His power, because turning Abraha's army into devoured straw was beyond the power of the Arabs' pelting alone, and His power achieved this under the cover of their pelting. We cited the eyewitnesses' report that a stone-carrying wind also blew, which occurred when the Arabs pelted them from Muhassir. At the Battle of the Trench, the wind likewise helped the Muslims. 
An important question: If the army was destroyed by the Arabs' pelting and the wind, and the birds came only to feed on the bodies, why was the order of the text: (And He sent upon them birds in flocks, pelting them with stones...) instead of: (pelting them... and He turned them into devoured straw, and He sent upon them birds in flocks)? Those who raise this question are unfamiliar with the Arabic style where, to show the immediate occurrence of a result, the effect is mentioned before the details of the action. The Qur'an uses this style to show the immediate acceptance of supplications. Here, we present an example from Surah Nuh:
(Noah said, 'My Lord, indeed they have disobeyed me and followed him whose wealth and children will not increase him except in loss. And they planned a mighty plot. And said, "Never leave your gods and never leave Wadd or Suwa' or Yaghuth and Ya'uq and Nasr." And they have led many astray. And [my Lord], do not increase the wrongdoers except in deviation.' Because of their sins they were drowned and put into the Fire, and they found not for themselves besides Allah any helpers. And Noah said, 'My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers a companion. Indeed, if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants and will not beget except wicked disbelievers.') (Nuh, 71:21-27).
Note that after the first statement of Noah's supplication, the end of his people is mentioned, while the rest of his supplication is postponed, although the end occurred after the entire supplication. This was done to show the immediate acceptance of his supplication. Similarly, in this Surah, to show the end of Abraha's hosts, the sending of the birds was mentioned first. Since the Surah is a favor to the Quraysh, the end of the enemy had to be displayed first.
Our teacher wrote a highly detailed commentary on this Surah, and for the sake of brevity, I have omitted some of his research, though it is highly important. He identified the 'pelting of pebbles' (ramy al-jamarat) during the Hajj as a monument of this 'pelting', and presented other important research. I advise the readers of this book to read his commentary as well.
